inline_review:
commit 041552ead4aa2ff4d5eeb115e0867baff8f43a6b
Author: Bing Jiao <bingjiao@google.com>

mm/vmscan: add demote= option to proactive reclaim

This commit adds a demote option to proactive memory reclaim to allow users
to control whether pages can be demoted to a lower memory tier. It changes
the default behavior to disable demotion.

> --- a/mm/vmscan.c
> +++ b/mm/vmscan.c
> @@ -7890,6 +7892,7 @@ int user_proactive_reclaim(char *buf,
>  			   struct mem_cgroup *memcg, pg_data_t *pgdat)
>  {
>  	unsigned int nr_retries = MAX_RECLAIM_RETRIES;
> +	unsigned int allow_demotion = 0;

Does this introduce a userspace interface regression by changing the default
behavior?

Prior to this patch, user_proactive_reclaim() allowed demoting pages to
slower memory tiers by default. Unconditionally disabling demotion could
break existing scripts and daemons. On swapless tiered systems, this might
cause anonymous memory reclaim to fail instead of successfully demoting
pages.

Also, does this break the expected semantics of node-level proactive
reclaim? Userspace tiering daemons rely on the node-level interface to
migrate cold pages from a specific fast node to a slower tier. By
disabling demotion by default, it seems node reclaim will now discard or
swap out pages instead of migrating them.

[ ... ]
> @@ -7922,6 +7925,10 @@ int user_proactive_reclaim(char *buf,
>  		case MEMORY_RECLAIM_SWAPPINESS_MAX:
>  			swappiness = SWAPPINESS_ANON_ONLY;
>  			break;
> +		case MEMORY_RECLAIM_ALLOW_DEMOTION:
> +			if (match_uint(&args[0], &allow_demotion))
> +				return -EINVAL;
> +			break;

Since this option is intended to be a boolean flag, could this loose input
validation cause future compatibility issues?

The parser accepts any valid unsigned integer, meaning a user passing
demote=2 will have allow_demotion set to 2, which evaluates as true. If
the demote option is later expanded to accept varying levels of
aggressiveness, existing scripts passing non-standard values might break.
Would it be safer to explicitly reject values greater than 1 here?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"UAPI regression and unintended behavior change for node-level and cgroup proactive reclaim.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. Prior to this patch, `user_proactive_reclaim()` did not set `no_demotion = 1` (or `MEMCG_RECLAIM_NO_DEMOTION`), meaning both cgroup `memory.reclaim` and node-level `/sys/devices/system/node/nodeX/reclaim` allowed demoting pages to slower memory tiers by default.
2. This patch introduces `unsigned int allow_demotion = 0;`, which unconditionally disables demotion for both interfaces unless explicitly requested via `demote=1`.
3. Changing the default behavior of an existing UAPI breaks backwards compatibility for userspace scripts and daemons that rely on `echo 10M > memory.reclaim` to demote pages. On swapless tiered systems, this will cause anonymous memory reclaim to completely fail with `-EAGAIN` instead of successfully demoting pages.
4. Furthermore, this change breaks the semantics of node-level proactive reclaim. While the commit message justifies the change by comparing cgroup reclaim to `memory.high` (where reducing overall memory footprint is the goal), node-level reclaim is used by userspace tiering daemons precisely to migrate cold pages from a specific fast node to a slower NUMA tier. By disabling demotion, node reclaim will now discard or swap out pages instead of migrating them, defeating its primary purpose. To prevent ABI breakage, `allow_demotion` must default to 1, or at the very least, demotion must remain the default for the node-level interface."},{"problem":"Loose input validation for the 'demote=' option allows arbitrary integers.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"1. The `demote=` option is designed and documented as a boolean flag (0 or 1).
2. However, the parser uses `match_uint(&args[0], &allow_demotion)`, which accepts any valid unsigned integer. For example, a user passing `demote=2` will have `allow_demotion` set to 2. Because the code evaluates `!allow_demotion`, any non-zero value acts as true and enables demotion, behaving identically to `demote=1`.
3. Failing to strictly restrict this input to 0 or 1 is poor API hygiene for new sysfs/cgroup interfaces. It creates a future backward compatibility hazard if the `demote` option is later expanded to accept varying levels of aggressiveness (e.g., where `demote=2` has a distinct meaning). The input should be explicitly validated (e.g., `if (allow_demotion > 1) return -EINVAL;`)."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 041552ead4aa2ff4d5eeb115e0867baff8f43a6b

[ ... ]
> @@ -7922,6 +7925,10 @@ int user_proactive_reclaim(char *buf,
>  		case MEMORY_RECLAIM_SWAPPINESS_MAX:

Since this option is intended to be a boolean flag, could this loose input

The parser accepts any valid unsigned integer, meaning a user passing

Would it be safer to explicitly reject values greater than 1 here?
